_note: ORIGINAL content. Think C++ and the Google C++ Class outline informed coverage only; no prose, examples, or exercises were copied.
title: Algorithm Problems: Part 1
summary: Order initialization, traversal, update, and return.
refresher: Define the input and output, trace a tiny case, initialize an accumulator, update it for each relevant element, and return after traversal completes.

blocks.body: Define the input and output, trace a tiny case, initialize an accumulator, update it for each relevant element, and return after traversal completes. Order initialization, traversal, update, and return.

Translate an algorithm prompt into input, output, remembered state, and repeated work. In a sum, the accumulator starts at zero because adding zero changes nothing. A range-based loop then adds each value exactly once, and the function returns only after traversal.

blocks.caption: The accumulator starts at zero and receives every value before it is returned.
Keep the return outside the loop body.

blocks.body: For `{3, 5, 2}`, total becomes three, then eight, then ten. An empty vector performs no updates and returns the initialized zero. Tracing both a normal case and the smallest valid case exposes misplaced initialization and return statements.

blocks.title: Inspect this state
blocks.body: Returning inside the range loop stops after one element; the final answer belongs after the complete traversal.

blocks.prompt: What is the sum accumulator initialized to?
blocks.why: Zero is the additive identity and works correctly even when the vector is empty.

blocks.prompt: Where does a full traversal return?
blocks.why: Later elements can change the result, so the loop must finish first.
